input.headline: How Guinness World Records began 7 decades ago

raw.headline: How Guinness World Records began 7 decades ago

winner: When Guinness World Records started, they were just trying to make a point, but they ended up making everyone else a record.

candidates.text: I tried to set a world record for shortest time to drink a milkshake, but the judge said I had to wait until Guinness World Records turned 70 first — apparently, they’re still celebrating their own birthday.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on the headline with a specific reference to Guinness World Records' age, concise and natural humor."}

candidates.text: I tried to break the world record for most beers consumed without puking, but they disqualified me for *overwhelming* performance.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat funny with a play on 'overwhelming performance,' but it lacks specificity to the headline and feels slightly generic. It also misses the historical context of Guinness World Records' origin."}

candidates.text: How did Guinness World Records begin? With a few beers and a lot of "record-breaking" excuses.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat relevant and uses a play on 'record-breaking' which ties to the topic, but it's generic and lacks specificity. The humor is mild and not particularly surprising or original."}

candidates.text: I always wanted to break a world record, so I decided to start my own — turns out, all I needed was a bunch of people willing to stand in line for seven decades just to watch me fail.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat humorous with a clever twist on the headline, but it lacks specificity about Guinness World Records and feels slightly generic in its approach to failure."}

candidates.text: The Guinness family were so bored of record-breaking that they started a new sport—*not* breaking records, just *standing* on them and pretending not to notice when someone else tried.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is creative and has a surprising twist, but it's slightly anachronistic and doesn't accurately reflect the actual history of Guinness World Records, which was started to settle a bet, not out of boredom."}

candidates.text: When Guinness World Records started, they were just trying to make a point, but they ended up making everyone else a record.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat clever with the wordplay on 'making a point' and 'making everyone else a record,' but it lacks specificity about how Guinness World Records began and feels a bit generic in its humor."}

candidates.text: A man once tried to break the Guinness World Record for the longest time spent holding a single beer can. He made it to 4 minutes before realizing he was missing the most important part—taking the cap off.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat specific and has a mild surprise element, but the humor feels weak and relies on a predictable punchline. It also doesn't strongly tie into the headline about the origin of Guinness World Records."}

candidates.text: Guinness World Records began seventy years ago when a man tried to eat a loaf of bread so fast he actually became the record for "Fastest Bread Consumption," but the officials had to disqualify him because he was still technically inside the bakery.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the humor falls flat due to a weak punchline and an implausible scenario (being disqualified for still being inside the bakery). It also lacks strong comedic timing and feels slightly forced."}

candidates.text: Guinness World Records started seventy years ago to document the world's most extraordinary feats, but today it mostly serves as a way for people to prove that their hobby of "doing nothing" is actually an elite, record-breaking sport.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the humor feels a bit forced and generic in its comparison of 'doing nothing' to an elite sport. It also doesn't fully align with the historical context of Guinness World Records."}
